Do you know David R. Liu? You should. Invented base editing (2016) and prime editing (2019) — precision tools that rewrite DNA one letter.
Do you know Jennifer Doudna? You should. Co-discovered CRISPR-Cas9 as a programmable gene-editing tool (2012), winning the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
Do you know George Church? You should. Pioneer of genome reading, writing, and editing at massive scale.
Do you know Feng Zhang? You should. First to adapt CRISPR-Cas9 for use in human cells (2013).
Do you know Katalin Karikó? You should. Spent 30+ years developing modified mRNA technology despite repeated funding rejections.
Do you know Emmanuelle Charpentier? You should. Discovered tracrRNA — the key molecule that guides CRISPR-Cas9 to its target.
Do you know Pardis Sabeti? You should. Pioneered real-time genomic sequencing during the Ebola outbreak, tracking viral mutations as they happened.
Do you know Kizzmekia Corbett? You should. Led the scientific team that designed the Moderna COVID-19 mRNA vaccine's spike protein construct — the key.
Do you know Patrick Hsu? You should. Developing programmable RNA-targeting tools for neurological diseases.
Do you know Prashant Mali? You should. One of the first to demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing in human cells (2013, alongside Church).
Do you know Helen O'Neill? You should. Advancing mitochondrial gene editing and embryo genomics to prevent inherited mitochondrial diseases.
Do you know Neville Sanjana? You should. Developed genome-wide CRISPR screening libraries that systematically knock out every human gene to discover cancer vulnerabilities.
Do you know Demis Hassabis? You should. Led development of AlphaFold, which predicted the 3D structures of virtually all known proteins, earning the 2024.
Do you know Svante Pääbo? You should. Awarded the 2022 Nobel Prize for sequencing the Neanderthal genome and discovering the Denisovans, transforming our understanding.
Do you know Drew Weissman? You should. Shared the 2023 Nobel Prize for nucleoside base modifications enabling mRNA therapeutics that formed the basis of.
Do you know Victor Ambros? You should. Awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology for discovering microRNA and its fundamental role in gene regulation.
Do you know Gary Ruvkun? You should. Co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize for revealing that microRNA-mediated gene silencing is a universal mechanism of.
Do you know Carl June? You should. Pioneered chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, achieving complete remission in terminal leukemia patients using genetically reprogrammed immune cells.
Do you know Shinya Yamanaka? You should. Nobel laureate whose Yamanaka factors continue enabling breakthroughs including partial cellular reprogramming to reverse aging markers without.
Do you know Tu Youyou? You should. Her discovery of artemisinin saved millions of lives from malaria and her team continues developing next-generation antimalarial.
Do you know Sarah Gilbert? You should. Led the development of the Oxford-AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine and now pioneers platform technology for rapid vaccine deployment.
Do you know Ugur Sahin? You should. Co-developed the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine and is now advancing personalized mRNA cancer vaccines showing tumor regression in.

Do you know Ozlem Tureci? You should. Co-pioneered individualized neoantigen vaccines and leads clinical programs where mRNA-encoded tumor antigens prime patient-specific anti-cancer immune responses.
Do you know Michael Levin? You should. Demonstrated that bioelectric patterns can reprogram planaria and frog cells to grow entirely new anatomical structures by.
Do you know Ed Boyden? You should. Co-invented optogenetics and expansion microscopy, enabling precise optical control of neural circuits and nanoscale brain imaging that.
Do you know Fei-Fei Li? You should. Created ImageNet and leads Stanford HAI, driving ambient intelligence systems for hospital monitoring that reduce clinical errors.
Do you know Daphne Koller? You should. Founded insitro to apply machine learning to drug development, building cellular phenotypic models that predict clinical trial.
Do you know Cynthia Kenyon? You should. Discovered that single-gene mutations in the insulin signaling pathway can double the lifespan of C.
Do you know Jack Szostak? You should. Nobel laureate whose lab demonstrated non-enzymatic RNA replication in protocells, providing critical evidence for the RNA world.
Do you know Zhang Yongzhen? You should. First to publicly share the complete SARS-CoV-2 genome sequence enabling global vaccine development within days of the.
Do you know Derrick Rossi? You should. Demonstrated that modified mRNA could reprogram adult cells into pluripotent stem cells without viral vectors, co-founding Moderna.
Do you know John Jumper? You should. Led the AlphaFold2 project that solved the 50-year protein structure prediction challenge and was awarded the 2024.

David R. Liu: Invented base editing (2016) and prime editing (2019) — precision tools that rewrite DNA one letter at a time without cutting.

George Church: Pioneer of genome reading, writing, and editing at massive scale.
Feng Zhang: First to adapt CRISPR-Cas9 for use in human cells (2013).
Katalin Karikó: Spent 30+ years developing modified mRNA technology despite repeated funding rejections.
Emmanuelle Charpentier: Discovered tracrRNA — the key molecule that guides CRISPR-Cas9 to its target.
Pardis Sabeti: Pioneered real-time genomic sequencing during the Ebola outbreak, tracking viral mutations as they happened.

Patrick Hsu: Developing programmable RNA-targeting tools for neurological diseases.
Prashant Mali: One of the first to demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing in human cells (2013, alongside Church).
Helen O'Neill: Advancing mitochondrial gene editing and embryo genomics to prevent inherited mitochondrial diseases.
Neville Sanjana: Developed genome-wide CRISPR screening libraries that systematically knock out every human gene to discover cancer vulnerabilities.
Demis Hassabis: Led development of AlphaFold, which predicted the 3D structures of virtually all known proteins, earning the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
Svante Pääbo: Awarded the 2022 Nobel Prize for sequencing the Neanderthal genome and discovering the Denisovans, transforming our understanding of human evolution.

Victor Ambros: Awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology for discovering microRNA and its fundamental role in gene regulation across all multicellular organisms.
Gary Ruvkun: Co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize for revealing that microRNA-mediated gene silencing is a universal mechanism of post-transcriptional regulation.
Carl June: Pioneered chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, achieving complete remission in terminal leukemia patients using genetically reprogrammed immune cells.
Shinya Yamanaka: Nobel laureate whose Yamanaka factors continue enabling breakthroughs including partial cellular reprogramming to reverse aging markers without cancer risk.
Tu Youyou: Her discovery of artemisinin saved millions of lives from malaria and her team continues developing next-generation antimalarial compounds against resistant strains.
Sarah Gilbert: Led the development of the Oxford-AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine and now pioneers platform technology for rapid vaccine deployment against Disease X.

Ozlem Tureci: Co-pioneered individualized neoantigen vaccines and leads clinical programs where mRNA-encoded tumor antigens prime patient-specific anti-cancer immune responses.
Yoshinori Ohsumi: His Nobel-winning discovery of autophagy mechanisms now informs therapeutic strategies for neurodegeneration, cancer, and aging through controlled cellular self-renewal.
Michael Levin: Demonstrated that bioelectric patterns can reprogram planaria and frog cells to grow entirely new anatomical structures by manipulating voltage gradients.
Ed Boyden: Co-invented optogenetics and expansion microscopy, enabling precise optical control of neural circuits and nanoscale brain imaging that has transformed neuroscience.
Fei-Fei Li: Created ImageNet and leads Stanford HAI, driving ambient intelligence systems for hospital monitoring that reduce clinical errors by 30%.
Daphne Koller: Founded insitro to apply machine learning to drug development, building cellular phenotypic models that predict clinical trial outcomes from preclinical data.
Cynthia Kenyon: Discovered that single-gene mutations in the insulin signaling pathway can double the lifespan of C.
Jack Szostak: Nobel laureate whose lab demonstrated non-enzymatic RNA replication in protocells, providing critical evidence for the RNA world hypothesis of life's origins.
Zhang Yongzhen: First to publicly share the complete SARS-CoV-2 genome sequence enabling global vaccine development within days of the outbreak's identification.
Derrick Rossi: Demonstrated that modified mRNA could reprogram adult cells into pluripotent stem cells without viral vectors, co-founding Moderna based on this platform.
John Jumper: Led the AlphaFold2 project that solved the 50-year protein structure prediction challenge and was awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
Gladys Kalema-Zikusoka: Pioneered the One Health approach linking human and wildlife health, helping double the endangered mountain gorilla population through integrated community health programs.

Which has MORE? Neurons in your brain vs Stars in the Milky Way? Stars win! 86 billion neurons vs ~200–400 billion stars.

Which device has MORE sensors? Modern smartphone vs Self-driving car? Self-driving car! Autonomous cars use 30+ sensors: LIDAR, radar, cameras, ultrasonic, GPS, IMU, and more.
Which weighs MORE? All data on the internet vs A strawberry? Strawberry! All internet data stored as electrons weighs about 50 grams total.
Which organ is HEAVIER? Brain vs Both lungs combined? Lungs! Both lungs: ~1.
Which happens MORE times per day? Blinks vs Heartbeats? Heartbeats! You blink ~15,000–20,000 times per day.
Which bone is STRONGER pound for pound? Human femur vs Concrete? Femur! Human bone is 4x stronger than concrete in compression.
Which has a LARGER surface area? Your lungs (all alveoli) vs Tennis court? Your lungs! If flattened, your lung alveoli cover ~70 square meters.
Which lasts LONGER in your body? A bone cell vs A brain neuron? Neuron! Most neurons last your entire lifetime — they're rarely replaced.
Which sense responds FASTER? Hearing vs Vision? Hearing! Auditory reaction time: ~150 milliseconds.
Which produces MORE saliva daily? Your mouth vs A kitchen faucet drip? Your mouth! You produce 0.
Which is LONGER? Your small intestine vs A bowling lane? Small intestine! Your small intestine is ~6 meters (20 feet) long.
Which has MORE sweat glands? Your feet vs Your palms? Your feet! Each foot has ~250,000 sweat glands producing up to half a pint.
Which contains MORE water? Your brain vs Your blood? Your blood! Blood is ~92% water by volume (total: ~5 liters).
Which grows FASTER? Fingernails vs Toenails? Fingernails! Fingernails grow ~3.
Which has MORE taste buds? A baby vs An adult? A baby! Babies have ~10,000 taste buds covering their tongue, cheeks, and throat.
Which carries signals FASTER? Myelinated nerve fiber vs Unmyelinated nerve fiber? Myelinated! Myelinated nerves: 70–120 m/s.
Which has a HIGHER surface area? Inside of your bones (trabecular) vs Cerebral cortex (unfolded)? Bone interior! Trabecular bone total surface area: ~7 square meters.
Which regenerates FASTER? Liver tissue vs Skin tissue? Liver! Your liver can regenerate to full size from just 25% remaining tissue in weeks.
Which is LONGER per cell? Stretched-out DNA vs Longest human nerve cell? Nerve cell! DNA in a cell: ~2 meters stretched out.
Which has MORE receptors? Your retina vs Your olfactory epithelium? Retina! Your retina has ~126 million photoreceptors.
Which is THICKER? Skin on your eyelid vs Skin on your heel? Heel skin! Eyelid skin: ~0.
Which blood type is RARER? AB negative vs O negative? AB negative! AB negative: only ~0.
Which system has MORE lymph nodes? Your neck region vs Your intestinal region? Intestinal region! Your gut-associated lymphoid tissue contains ~500 lymph nodes.
Which was published FIRST? Newton's Principia vs Darwin's Origin of Species? Newton! Newton's Principia Mathematica was published in 1687.
Which discovery happened FIRST? Electricity (Galvani's experiments) vs Electromagnetism (Oersted)? Electricity! Galvani's bioelectricity experiments were in 1780.
Which scientist held MORE patents? Thomas Edison vs Nikola Tesla? Edison! Edison held 1,093 US patents — the most of any individual at the time.
Which was discovered FIRST? The electron vs Radioactivity? Radioactivity! Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896.
Which experiment cost MORE? Large Hadron Collider vs Human Genome Project? LHC! The LHC cost ~$13.
Which theory was proposed FIRST? Atomic theory (Dalton) vs Wave theory of light (Young)? Atomic theory! Dalton proposed modern atomic theory in 1803.
Which happened FIRST? First vaccination vs Germ theory of disease? Vaccination! Jenner developed smallpox vaccination in 1796.
Who lived LONGER? Isaac Newton vs Albert Einstein? Newton! Newton lived 84 years (1642–1727).
Which element was isolated FIRST? Oxygen vs Hydrogen? Hydrogen! Hydrogen was first isolated by Cavendish in 1766.
Which was built FIRST? First electron microscope vs First radio broadcast? Radio! Fessenden's first radio broadcast was in 1906.
Which won the Nobel Prize YOUNGER? Marie Curie vs Lawrence Bragg? Bragg! Lawrence Bragg won the Nobel Prize in Physics at age 25 in.
Which was measured FIRST accurately? Speed of light vs Speed of sound? Speed of sound! The speed of sound was first accurately measured by.
Which scientific instrument is OLDER? Thermometer vs Barometer? Thermometer! Galileo invented the thermoscope around 1592.
Which space achievement came FIRST? Animal in orbit (Laika) vs Human in space (Gagarin)? Laika! Laika orbited Earth on Sputnik 2 in November 1957.
Which periodic table element was predicted BEFORE discovery? Gallium vs Germanium? Gallium! Mendeleev predicted gallium (eka-aluminum) in 1871; it was discovered in 1875.
Which medical breakthrough happened FIRST? First antibiotic (penicillin) vs First X-ray image? X-ray! Rontgen took the first X-ray image in 1895.
Which was mapped FIRST? Structure of insulin vs Structure of hemoglobin? Insulin! Sanger determined insulin's amino acid sequence in 1951 — winning the Nobel Prize.
Which mathematical concept is OLDER? Zero as a number vs Pi calculation? Pi! Babylonians approximated pi around 1900 BC.
Which law was formulated FIRST? Law of gravity (Newton) vs Coulomb's law (electric force)? Gravity! Newton published the law of gravity in 1687.
Which was observed FIRST through a microscope? Bacteria vs Human cells? Human cells! Hooke observed cork cells in 1665.
Which particle was theorized FIRST? Neutrino vs Positron? Positron! Dirac predicted the positron (antimatter) in 1928.
Which organ can you donate while ALIVE? One lung lobe vs Part of your brain? Lung lobe! Living donors can give a lung lobe.
Which surgery was performed FIRST? Heart transplant vs Hip replacement? Hip replacement! The first hip replacement was performed in 1960 by Charnley.
Which heals FASTER? Broken rib vs Torn ACL? Broken rib! Ribs heal naturally in 6–8 weeks.
Which is MORE common worldwide? Nearsightedness (myopia) vs Tooth decay? Tooth decay! Tooth decay affects ~3.
Which medicine is OLDER? Aspirin (willow bark use) vs Morphine isolation? Aspirin! Willow bark (salicin/aspirin) was used by ancient Egyptians 3,500+ years ago.
Which has a HIGHER survival rate today? Heart attack vs Stroke? Heart attack! Heart attack 30-day survival rate: ~90–95%.
Which scan uses MORE radiation? Chest X-ray vs CT scan of the abdomen? CT scan! A chest X-ray delivers ~0.
Which vitamin deficiency is MORE common globally? Vitamin D deficiency vs Iron deficiency? Iron deficiency! Iron deficiency affects ~2 billion people worldwide.
Which antibiotic was discovered MORE recently? Teixobactin vs Daptomycin? Teixobactin! Teixobactin was discovered in 2015 using a novel soil-screening method.
Which procedure is SHORTER? LASIK eye surgery vs Wisdom tooth extraction? LASIK! LASIK takes ~15 minutes for both eyes, with the laser active for.
Which organ was transplanted FIRST successfully? Kidney vs Heart? Kidney! The first successful kidney transplant was in 1954 between identical twins by Dr.
Which vaccine was developed FASTER? COVID-19 mRNA vaccine vs Mumps vaccine? COVID-19 vaccine! COVID-19 mRNA vaccines took ~11 months from sequencing to authorization.
Which has a LONGER recovery time? Open heart surgery vs Brain surgery (craniotomy)? Heart surgery! Open heart surgery recovery: 6–12 weeks.
Which costs MORE per year of treatment? Type 1 diabetes management vs Hemophilia A treatment? Hemophilia A! Hemophilia A treatment: $300,000–$600,000+ per year.
Which causes MORE deaths annually? Diabetes vs Air pollution-related disease? Air pollution! Air pollution causes ~6.
Which test is MORE sensitive? PCR test vs Rapid antigen test? PCR! PCR can detect as few as 1–10 copies of viral RNA.
Which is MORE common in humans? Genetic mutations at birth vs Bacterial infections per year? Infections! Humans average ~70 new mutations at birth.
Which takes LONGER to develop? Average new drug vs New commercial airplane? New drug! Average drug development: 10–15 years and $2.
Which carries MORE oxygen? Hemoglobin in blood vs Dissolved oxygen in plasma? Hemoglobin! Hemoglobin carries 98.

Franklin published 37 papers in her short career. Quality over quantity, always.
Franklin's X-ray work was also crucial to understanding RNA viruses.
radioactivity discovery took over 60 hours of X-ray exposure. Patience and precision.
Franklin died at 37 from ovarian cancer, likely from X-ray exposure in her research.
The Nobel committee didn't consider Franklin because prizes aren't given posthumously.
Franklin's coal research during WWII helped develop better gas masks for soldiers.
the establishment that ignored Curie saw radioactivity discovery without Franklin's knowledge or permission.
Franklin independently concluded DNA was a radioactivity from her own data.
the Sorbonne Laboratory finally named a building after Franklin in 2001. About time.

The strong nuclear force is 100 times stronger than electromagnetism but only works at subatomic distances.
Gravitational time dilation is real. GPS satellites correct for it every single day.
Entropy always increases in an isolated system. That's why your room gets messy.
Photons are massless force carriers for electromagnetism. They're how light works.
About 95% of the universe is dark matter and dark energy. We're the 5% minority.
The double-slit experiment proved wave-particle duality. Single photons create interference patterns.
Quantum tunneling is how the Sun fuses hydrogen. It's not just theory — it powers our star.

Earth's magnetic field comes from convection currents in the liquid iron outer core.
Tectonic plates move 1 to 10 centimeters per year. Slow but unstoppable.
The Richter scale is logarithmic. Each whole number is 10x more ground motion.
The ozone layer absorbs 97 to 99 percent of the Sun's harmful UV radiation.
Absolute zero is minus 273.15 degrees Celsius. Molecular motion theoretically stops.
The Mpemba effect: hot water can freeze faster than cold water under certain conditions.
Graphene is one atom thick, 200 times stronger than steel, and conducts better than copper.
Superfluid helium flows with zero viscosity. It can climb walls and defy gravity.
An atom is 99.9999999999996 percent empty space. Solid matter is mostly nothing.

Ice is less dense than liquid water. That's why it floats and why lakes don't freeze solid.
Glass is an amorphous solid — molecules frozen in liquid-like disorder. Not a slow-flowing liquid.
Mitochondria: Mitochondria convert glucose into ATP through cellular respiration, providing energy for virtually every cellular process..
46: Humans have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs. Chimpanzees have 48 — one of our pairs fused during evolution!.
O negative: O negative has no A, B, or Rh antigens, so it won't trigger immune reactions in any recipient..
About 60%: We share ~60% of DNA with bananas! Many fundamental cellular processes are conserved across all life..
Liver: The liver can regenerate from as little as 25% of its tissue — the only internal organ that fully regenerates in mammals..
Egg cell (ovum): The egg cell is ~0.1mm — visible to the naked eye! Roughly the size of a period at the end of.
Earthworm (5): Earthworms have 5 pairs of aortic arches functioning as hearts, pumping blood through their segmented bodies..
Oxygen: Oxygen is ~65% of body mass, mostly because you're ~60% water (H₂O) and oxygen is the heavier atom..
Cell wall: Plant cells have a rigid cellulose cell wall outside the membrane, giving structural support. Animal cells only have a membrane.
About 2 meters: Each cell has ~2 meters of DNA packed incredibly tightly around histone proteins. All your cells' DNA would reach the Sun.
Eye muscles: The orbicularis oculi can contract in <1/100th of a second — that's why blinking is so fast..
Wood frog: Wood frogs produce glucose antifreeze. Their heart stops, they stop breathing, literally freeze — then thaw and hop away in spring!.
About 1,000: ~1,000 species totaling trillions of individual bacteria — your microbiome collectively weighs about 2 kg..
Horseshoe crab: Horseshoe crabs use copper-based hemocyanin (blue) instead of iron hemoglobin. Their blood tests all medical equipment for bacterial contamination!.
Programmed cell death: Apoptosis is your body's clean self-destruct for damaged cells. ~50–70 billion cells die this way every single day!.
Fungi: Fungi are their own kingdom — more closely related to animals than plants! They don't photosynthesize; they digest food externally..
Femur: The femur supports 30x your body weight. Pound for pound, bone is stronger than steel and more flexible than concrete.
About 10,000: ~10,000 taste buds with 50–100 receptor cells each. They regenerate every 10–14 days.
Bat: Bats are the only true-flying mammals. Over 1,400 species — about 20% of all mammal species!.
Iron in hemoglobin: Iron in hemoglobin reflects red light when oxygenated. Deoxygenated blood is darker red (never blue!).
About 100,000: ~100,000 beats daily pumping ~2,000 gallons of blood. Over a lifetime: ~2.
Red blood cells: Mature RBCs eject their nucleus for more hemoglobin. They can't divide or repair, living only ~120 days.
Protein synthesis: Ribosomes read mRNA and assemble amino acids into proteins. Each cell has millions — the factories of life.
Skin: Skin covers ~20 sq ft, weighs ~8 lbs. Three complex layers with nerves, blood vessels, and glands.
Greenland shark: Greenland sharks can live 400+ years! They grow ~1 cm/year and don't mature until ~150 years old..
7.35–7.45 (alkaline): Blood is slightly alkaline. Even small changes are life-threatening — multiple buffer systems keep pH precisely regulated.
About 60%: Adults are ~60% water. Babies ~75%.
About 20,000: ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes. A grape has more (~30,000)! Gene count doesn't equal complexity.
Vitamin D: UV-B converts a cholesterol derivative into vitamin D3 — crucial for calcium absorption and bone health..
Stapes: The stapes (stirrup) in the middle ear is only 3mm — about the size of a grain of rice..
Protect against UV: Melanin absorbs UV radiation protecting DNA. More melanin = darker skin = more UV protection.
Planaria flatworm: Planaria regenerate from 1/279th of their body! Adult stem cells (neoblasts) throughout their body make this possible..
Hydrogen: Hydrogen bonds between base pairs (A-T, G-C). Individually weak but collectively strong — and easy to unzip for replication.
Tooth enamel: Enamel is 96% mineral — harder than steel! But it can't regenerate, which is why cavities are permanent..
Up to 120 m/s: Motor neurons reach 120 m/s (268 mph). Myelin sheaths insulate nerves and speed up transmission.
Mitochondria: Mitochondria have circular DNA inherited only from your mother — supporting the endosymbiotic theory that they were once free-living bacteria!.
Blue whale: Blue whales reach 100ft and 200 tons — larger than any dinosaur! Heart weighs ~400 lbs..
Keratin: Keratin also makes hooves, horns, claws, feathers — all modified versions of the same tough fibrous protein..
37 trillion: ~37 trillion cells. Plus ~38 trillion bacteria — slightly outnumbering your own cells!.
Motor coordination: The cerebellum has more than half of all brain neurons! It fine-tunes movement, balance, and motor learning..
Platelets: Platelets clump at wound sites and trigger a cascade forming a fibrin mesh to stop bleeding..
A gene-editing tool: CRISPR-Cas9 is adapted from a bacterial immune system. It lets scientists make precise DNA cuts for targeted gene modification.
Smell: The olfactory bulb connects directly to the amygdala and hippocampus — that's why a smell can trigger vivid memories instantly..
About 600: ~600 skeletal muscles making up ~40% of body weight. The gluteus maximus is largest; the stapedius (ear) is smallest.
tRNA: Transfer RNA acts as an adapter — one end reads the mRNA codon, the other carries the matching amino acid..
4: 4 chambers: 2 atria (receiving) and 2 ventricles (pumping). This separates oxygenated and deoxygenated blood completely.
Medulla oblongata: The medulla controls breathing, heart rate, and blood pressure automatically. Damage here is usually fatal.
Endorphins: Endorphins bind to opioid receptors, reducing pain and producing euphoria during sustained exercise..
Transcription: Transcription: RNA polymerase reads DNA and builds complementary mRNA, which then travels to ribosomes for protein synthesis..
4: Frontal (decisions), parietal (sensation), temporal (hearing), and occipital (vision). 4 lobes x 2 hemispheres = 8 total.
Protozoan parasite: Plasmodium parasites transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. They infect red blood cells, affecting ~250 million people yearly.
Aorta: The aorta is ~1 inch diameter, carrying oxygenated blood from heart to entire body. Blood pressure is highest here.
Phospholipids: The phospholipid bilayer: hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails forming a flexible selective barrier..
About 20 seconds: ~20 seconds per circuit. In its 120-day life, one RBC makes ~500,000 trips around your body!.
Helicase: Helicase breaks hydrogen bonds between base pairs, 'unzipping' the radioactivity at ~10,000 RPM!.
Tropical rainforest: Rainforests cover 6% of Earth but contain 50%+ of all species. The Amazon alone has ~10% of all species.
Filter blood & recycle RBCs: The spleen filters blood, removing old RBCs and recycling iron. It also stores white blood cells for emergencies.
Hemoglobin: Each hemoglobin has 4 iron atoms binding one O₂ each. One RBC has ~270 million hemoglobin molecules!.
Tendon: Tendons are dense collagen fibers. The Achilles tendon withstands 1,000+ lbs of force during running.
AB negative: AB negative is found in <1% of people. But AB positive is the universal plasma donor.
Adrenaline: Adrenaline from adrenal glands: heart rate up, blood to muscles, senses sharpened — all in seconds..
Cones: ~6 million cones in 3 types (red, green, blue). Mantis shrimp have 16 types — imagine their color vision!.
Autotroph: Autotrophs include plants, algae, and some bacteria — converting inorganic molecules into food via photosynthesis..
Motor neuron: Motor neurons stretch from spinal cord to toe tip — over 1 meter long! By far the longest cells..
Hypothalamus: The hypothalamus: hunger, thirst, temperature, sleep, hormones — your homeostasis center, only almond-sized..
Nucleic acids: DNA stores the blueprint; RNA reads and executes it. Together they're the information system of all known life.
Unrelated species evolving similar traits: Wings evolved independently in birds, bats, and insects. Similar environments produce similar solutions.
300 million: ~300 million alveoli = ~70 sq meters surface area. That's a tennis court folded inside your chest.
Both together: Insulin lowers sugar; glucagon raises it. Together they maintain the precise balance your body needs.
10+ billion: Through V(D)J recombination, your immune system generates 10+ billion unique antibodies — enough to recognize virtually anything..
Collagen: Collagen is 25–35% of total protein — the structural scaffold of skin, bones, tendons. Your body's biological glue.
Vitamin K: 'K' stands for 'Koagulation' (German). Newborns get a vitamin K injection to prevent bleeding disorders.
Smooth ER: The smooth ER detoxifies drugs, alcohol, and waste — especially in liver cells. Also synthesizes lipids.
Deoxyribose: Deoxyribose is ribose minus one oxygen ('deoxy'). This makes DNA more stable than RNA — perfect for long-term storage.
A dome-shaped breathing muscle: When the diaphragm contracts and flattens, it creates negative pressure drawing air into your lungs..
~5,000 years: A bristlecone pine named Methuselah is 5,000+ years old — already ancient when the Egyptian pyramids were built..
1796: Edward Jenner created the smallpox vaccine in 1796 using cowpox material — the word 'vaccine' comes from 'vacca' (cow in Latin)..
Smallpox: Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980 after a global vaccination campaign. It killed ~300 million people in the 20th century alone before eradication.

David R. Liu at Broad Institute of MIT & Harvard: Invented base editing (2016) and prime editing (2019) — precision tools that rewrite DNA.
Jennifer Doudna at UC Berkeley / Innovative Genomics Institute: Co-discovered CRISPR-Cas9 as a programmable gene-editing tool (2012), winning the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
George Church at Harvard Medical School / Wyss Institute: Pioneer of genome reading, writing, and editing at massive scale.
Feng Zhang at Broad Institute of MIT & Harvard: First to adapt CRISPR-Cas9 for use in human cells (2013).
Katalin Karikó at University of Szeged / BioNTech: Spent 30+ years developing modified mRNA technology despite repeated funding rejections.
Emmanuelle Charpentier at Max Planck Unit for the Science of Pathogens, Berlin: Discovered tracrRNA — the key molecule that guides CRISPR-Cas9 to its target.
Pardis Sabeti at Broad Institute / Harvard: Pioneered real-time genomic sequencing during the Ebola outbreak, tracking viral mutations as they happened.
Patrick Hsu at UC Berkeley / Arc Institute: Developing programmable RNA-targeting tools for neurological diseases.
Prashant Mali at UC San Diego: One of the first to demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing in human cells (2013, alongside Church).
Helen O'Neill at University College London (UCL): Advancing mitochondrial gene editing and embryo genomics to prevent inherited mitochondrial diseases.
Neville Sanjana at New York Genome Center / NYU: Developed genome-wide CRISPR screening libraries that systematically knock out every human gene to discover cancer vulnerabilities.
Demis Hassabis at Google DeepMind: Led development of AlphaFold, which predicted the 3D structures of virtually all known proteins, earning the 2024 Nobel Prize.
Drew Weissman at University of Pennsylvania: Shared the 2023 Nobel Prize for nucleoside base modifications enabling mRNA therapeutics that formed the basis of COVID-19 vaccines.
Gary Ruvkun at Harvard Medical School: Co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize for revealing that microRNA-mediated gene silencing is a universal mechanism of post-transcriptional regulation.
Carl June at University of Pennsylvania: Pioneered chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, achieving complete remission in terminal leukemia patients using genetically reprogrammed immune cells.
Sarah Gilbert at University of Oxford: Led the development of the Oxford-AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine and now pioneers platform technology for rapid vaccine deployment against.
Ugur Sahin at BioNTech: Co-developed the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine and is now advancing personalized mRNA cancer vaccines showing tumor regression in melanoma and pancreatic cancer.
Ozlem Tureci at BioNTech: Co-pioneered individualized neoantigen vaccines and leads clinical programs where mRNA-encoded tumor antigens prime patient-specific anti-cancer immune responses.
Michael Levin at Tufts University: Demonstrated that bioelectric patterns can reprogram planaria and frog cells to grow entirely new anatomical structures by manipulating voltage gradients.
Fei-Fei Li at Stanford University: Created ImageNet and leads Stanford HAI, driving ambient intelligence systems for hospital monitoring that reduce clinical errors by 30%.
Daphne Koller at insitro: Founded insitro to apply machine learning to drug development, building cellular phenotypic models that predict clinical trial outcomes from preclinical data.
Cynthia Kenyon at Calico Labs / UCSF: Discovered that single-gene mutations in the insulin signaling pathway can double the lifespan of C.
Jack Szostak at University of Chicago: Nobel laureate whose lab demonstrated non-enzymatic RNA replication in protocells, providing critical evidence for the RNA world hypothesis.
Zhang Yongzhen at Fudan University: First to publicly share the complete SARS-CoV-2 genome sequence enabling global vaccine development within days of the outbreak's identification.
John Jumper at Google DeepMind: Led the AlphaFold2 project that solved the 50-year protein structure prediction challenge and was awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize.

David R. Liu — Invented base editing (2016) and prime editing (2019) — precision tools that rewrite DNA one letter at a time without.
Jennifer Doudna — Co-discovered CRISPR-Cas9 as a programmable gene-editing tool (2012), winning the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Just incredible work.
George Church — Pioneer of genome reading, writing, and editing at massive scale. Just incredible work.
Feng Zhang — First to adapt CRISPR-Cas9 for use in human cells (2013). Just incredible work.
Katalin Karikó — Spent 30+ years developing modified mRNA technology despite repeated funding rejections. Just incredible work.
Emmanuelle Charpentier — Discovered tracrRNA — the key molecule that guides CRISPR-Cas9 to its target. Just incredible work.
Pardis Sabeti — Pioneered real-time genomic sequencing during the Ebola outbreak, tracking viral mutations as they happened. Just incredible work.
Kizzmekia Corbett — Led the scientific team that designed the Moderna COVID-19 mRNA vaccine's spike protein construct — the key antigen that trained billions.
Patrick Hsu — Developing programmable RNA-targeting tools for neurological diseases. Just incredible work.
Prashant Mali — One of the first to demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing in human cells (2013, alongside Church). Just incredible work.
Helen O'Neill — Advancing mitochondrial gene editing and embryo genomics to prevent inherited mitochondrial diseases. Just incredible work.
Neville Sanjana — Developed genome-wide CRISPR screening libraries that systematically knock out every human gene to discover cancer vulnerabilities. Just incredible work.
Demis Hassabis — Led development of AlphaFold, which predicted the 3D structures of virtually all known proteins, earning the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
Svante Pääbo — Awarded the 2022 Nobel Prize for sequencing the Neanderthal genome and discovering the Denisovans, transforming our understanding of human evolution. Just.
Drew Weissman — Shared the 2023 Nobel Prize for nucleoside base modifications enabling mRNA therapeutics that formed the basis of COVID-19 vaccines. Just incredible work.
Victor Ambros — Awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology for discovering microRNA and its fundamental role in gene regulation across all multicellular organisms.
Gary Ruvkun — Co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize for revealing that microRNA-mediated gene silencing is a universal mechanism of post-transcriptional regulation. Just incredible work.
Carl June — Pioneered chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, achieving complete remission in terminal leukemia patients using genetically reprogrammed immune cells. Just incredible work.
Shinya Yamanaka — Nobel laureate whose Yamanaka factors continue enabling breakthroughs including partial cellular reprogramming to reverse aging markers without cancer risk. Just incredible work.
Tu Youyou — Her discovery of artemisinin saved millions of lives from malaria and her team continues developing next-generation antimalarial compounds against resistant strains.
Sarah Gilbert — Led the development of the Oxford-AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine and now pioneers platform technology for rapid vaccine deployment against Disease X. Just.
Ugur Sahin — Co-developed the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine and is now advancing personalized mRNA cancer vaccines showing tumor regression in melanoma and pancreatic cancer.
Ozlem Tureci — Co-pioneered individualized neoantigen vaccines and leads clinical programs where mRNA-encoded tumor antigens prime patient-specific anti-cancer immune responses. Just incredible work.
Michael Levin — Demonstrated that bioelectric patterns can reprogram planaria and frog cells to grow entirely new anatomical structures by manipulating voltage gradients. Just.
Ed Boyden — Co-invented optogenetics and expansion microscopy, enabling precise optical control of neural circuits and nanoscale brain imaging that has transformed neuroscience. Just.
Fei-Fei Li — Created ImageNet and leads Stanford HAI, driving ambient intelligence systems for hospital monitoring that reduce clinical errors by 30%. Just incredible work.
Daphne Koller — Founded insitro to apply machine learning to drug development, building cellular phenotypic models that predict clinical trial outcomes from preclinical data.
Cynthia Kenyon — Discovered that single-gene mutations in the insulin signaling pathway can double the lifespan of C. Just incredible work.
Jack Szostak — Nobel laureate whose lab demonstrated non-enzymatic RNA replication in protocells, providing critical evidence for the RNA world hypothesis of life's origins.
Zhang Yongzhen — First to publicly share the complete SARS-CoV-2 genome sequence enabling global vaccine development within days of the outbreak's identification. Just incredible work.
Derrick Rossi — Demonstrated that modified mRNA could reprogram adult cells into pluripotent stem cells without viral vectors, co-founding Moderna based on this platform.
John Jumper — Led the AlphaFold2 project that solved the 50-year protein structure prediction challenge and was awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

Invented base editing (2016) and prime editing (2019) — precision tools that rewrite DNA one letter at a time without cutting. David R. Liu.
Co-discovered CRISPR-Cas9 as a programmable gene-editing tool (2012), winning the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Jennifer Doudna is rewriting the rules.
Pioneer of genome reading, writing, and editing at massive scale. George Church is rewriting the rules.
First to adapt CRISPR-Cas9 for use in human cells (2013). Feng Zhang is rewriting the rules.
Spent 30+ years developing modified mRNA technology despite repeated funding rejections. Katalin Karikó is rewriting the rules.
Discovered tracrRNA — the key molecule that guides CRISPR-Cas9 to its target. Emmanuelle Charpentier is rewriting the rules.
Pioneered real-time genomic sequencing during the Ebola outbreak, tracking viral mutations as they happened. Pardis Sabeti is rewriting the rules.
Led the scientific team that designed the Moderna COVID-19 mRNA vaccine's spike protein construct — the key antigen that trained billions of immune systems.
Developing programmable RNA-targeting tools for neurological diseases. Patrick Hsu is rewriting the rules.
One of the first to demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing in human cells (2013, alongside Church). Prashant Mali is rewriting the rules.
Advancing mitochondrial gene editing and embryo genomics to prevent inherited mitochondrial diseases. Helen O'Neill is rewriting the rules.

Lawrence Bragg won the Nobel Prize in Physics at age 25 in 1915 — the youngest science Nobel laureate ever. Did NOT see that coming.
The speed of sound was first accurately measured by Marin Mersenne in 1630. Did NOT see that coming.
Galileo invented the thermoscope around 1592. Did NOT see that coming.
Laika orbited Earth on Sputnik 2 in November 1957. Did NOT see that coming.
Mendeleev predicted gallium (eka-aluminum) in 1871; it was discovered in 1875. Did NOT see that coming.
Rontgen took the first X-ray image in 1895. Did NOT see that coming.
Sanger determined insulin's amino acid sequence in 1951 — winning the Nobel Prize. Did NOT see that coming.
Babylonians approximated pi around 1900 BC. Did NOT see that coming.
Newton published the law of gravity in 1687. Did NOT see that coming.
Hooke observed cork cells in 1665. Did NOT see that coming.
Wohler synthesized urea in 1828 — the first organic compound made from inorganic materials, shattering 'vitalism. Did NOT see that coming.
Dirac predicted the positron (antimatter) in 1928. Did NOT see that coming.
Living donors can give a lung lobe, kidney, or part of their liver. Did NOT see that coming.
Malaria has killed an estimated 50+ billion people throughout human history — nearly half of all humans who ever lived. Did NOT see that coming.
The first hip replacement was performed in 1960 by Charnley. Did NOT see that coming.
Ribs heal naturally in 6–8 weeks. Did NOT see that coming.
Tooth decay affects ~3. Did NOT see that coming.
Willow bark (salicin/aspirin) was used by ancient Egyptians 3,500+ years ago. Did NOT see that coming.
Heart attack 30-day survival rate: ~90–95%. Did NOT see that coming.
A chest X-ray delivers ~0. Did NOT see that coming.
Iron deficiency affects ~2 billion people worldwide. Did NOT see that coming.
Teixobactin was discovered in 2015 using a novel soil-screening method. Did NOT see that coming.
LASIK takes ~15 minutes for both eyes, with the laser active for only 20–50 seconds per eye. Did NOT see that coming.
The first successful kidney transplant was in 1954 between identical twins by Dr. Did NOT see that coming.
COVID-19 mRNA vaccines took ~11 months from sequencing to authorization. Did NOT see that coming.
Open heart surgery recovery: 6–12 weeks. Did NOT see that coming.
Hemophilia A treatment: $300,000–$600,000+ per year. Did NOT see that coming.
Air pollution causes ~6. Did NOT see that coming.
PCR can detect as few as 1–10 copies of viral RNA. Did NOT see that coming.
Humans average ~70 new mutations at birth. Did NOT see that coming.
Average drug development: 10–15 years and $2. Did NOT see that coming.

The fact that "Humans and dinosaurs lived at the same time" persists despite Radiometric dating is remarkable.
The fact that "Microwave ovens destroy nutrients and cause cancer" persists despite Harvard Health Publishing is remarkable.
The fact that "mRNA vaccines change your DNA" persists despite CDC is remarkable.
The fact that "Essential oils cure diseases" persists despite Cochrane Reviews is remarkable.
